findings.summary: Reserved admin/inspector endpoints (/messages, /connect, /disconnect) are gated only by a spoofable loopback check ("127.0.0.1" not in scope["client"]) while uvicorn runs with forwarded_allow_ips="*", so a remote attacker sending an X-Forwarded-For: 127.0.0.1 header bypasses the guard and reaches the admin surface.

Inbound signed envelopes have no replay or expiry enforcement — asgi.py and mailbox.py verify the signature but never check env.expires against current time and keep no nonce/session dedup, so a captured valid envelope can be replayed and re-dispatched indefinitely, violating the remit's explicit anti-replay rule.

Agent identity and wallet private keys are written to a plaintext private_keys.json in the working directory via storage.save_private_keys/get_or_create_private_keys (triggered by Agent(name=...) without a seed), directly violating the remit prohibition on writing key material to disk in plaintext.

The ASGI server binds to a hardcoded HOST="0.0.0.0" (all interfaces) with no operator opt-in, so every agent is publicly reachable by default even though the remit requires human approval before binding a non-loopback interface.

The A2A inbound bridge (AgentverseAgentExecutor) performs no request validation (_validate_request always returns False), correlates responses to pending requests by sender address alone, and carries per-user identity as a spoofable inline [USER_CONTEXT:id] string in the message body, allowing a caller to impersonate another user's context.

Envelopes whose sender begins with the "user" prefix skip signature verification entirely (asgi.py: if not is_user_address(env.sender): env.verify()), so the sender identity passed to handlers is attacker-chooseable and the framework gives handlers no signal that it is unauthenticated.

The /agent_info inspector endpoint is explicitly excluded from the loopback guard and is enabled by default (enable_agent_inspector=True), exposing agent address, endpoints, protocols, metadata, and port to unauthenticated remote callers despite the remit classing inspector endpoints as local-operator-only.

The official experimental chat_agent example evaluates a remote message field with eval(msg.expression), teaching an unauthenticated-input-to-code-execution pattern that a deployed agent using the example would expose.

The framework has no structured, action-level audit trail over consequential events (identity/key generation, Almanac registration, /connect and /disconnect, signature-verification failures) — only free-form warning/info logging — leaving admin and identity actions with no durable oversight record.

Core dependencies are floor-pinned with unbounded upper ranges (e.g. uagents-core >=0.4.6, cosmpy >=0.12.1) leaving the runtime open to version-swap/dependency-confusion drift, though poetry.lock/uv.lock are committed.

get_or_create_private_keys returns one freshly generated wallet key but persists a different random wallet key (save_private_keys(name, identity_key, PrivateKey().private_key)), so a name-persisted agent's wallet address silently changes between first run and restart, violating the remit's restart-stability expectation for on-chain identity.

The ASGI server sets a wildcard CORS policy (Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *, permissive methods/headers) on all agent HTTP responses; impact is limited by credentials=false and signature-verified /submit, but it broadens browser-originated cross-origin access to the agent surface.
